name: AltID procurement language — codify platform-neutral access and fallback requirements in AltID-related procurements
consequences: Embedding platform-neutral criteria into procurement creates immediate leverage to diversify supplier risk and guard against reliance on single mobile ecosystems. Downstream, it can raise negotiation complexity, extend lead times, and demand stronger governance to enforce compliance across agencies.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into procurement contracts, with explicit interoperability criteria, review milestones, and penalties for non-compliance.

Adopt a dual-sourcing procurement approach that allocates baseline AltID integration work to a government-approved neutral supplier while permitting pilot support from existing platform vendors under strict risk controls.
Create a procurement sandbox that funds exploratory platform-neutral fallback components with strict budget caps and mandated shareable technical documentation for EU review.
review: Core tension centers on aligning procurement reform with ongoing MitID certification cycles, while maintaining service continuity. A key weakness is that none of the options fully account for incumbent resistance within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator to any shift away from Apple/Google trust anchors.

name: Dependency mapping — formalize the MitID surface and AltID touchpoints across platforms
lever_classification: methodology — which data collection approach to use
consequences: Mapping clarifies where platform trust signals currently anchor identity flows and where gaps could enable platform-neutral pathways. The direct effect is improved decision readiness; downstream, the effort may reveal gaps requiring policy shifts, funding, or new standards interventions, potentially slowing initial momentum.
options: Develop a comprehensive MitID dependency surface map using threat modeling, stakeholder interviews, and interoperability tests across AltID, ensuring traceable baselines.
Establish a quantitative scoring framework for platform-neutral readiness based on interoperability, privacy, procurement lead times, vendor resilience, and governance alignment.
Conduct a rapid, cross-stakeholder workshop series to converge on risk tolerances and decision gates for platform-neutral fallback adoption.
review: Core tension: measurement and surface mapping may become bureaucratic bottlenecks without rapid execution; weakness: data gaps from platform telemetry and vendor transparency could limit scoring accuracy.

name: Phase sequencing — structure demonstrations and policy engagement to de-risk platform-neutral fallback
lever_classification: execution — how to sequence the rollout phases
consequences: A disciplined phase sequence reduces the risk of over-claiming feasibility and aligns technical work with policy milestones. Downstream, this may slow aggressive timelines and require flexible funding gates if policy uptake lags, but increases credibility with stakeholders.
options: Sequence phase activities to prioritize non-production demonstrators before policy negotiations, ensuring tangible evidence supports procurement language.
Run parallel EU alignment tracks while phase two demonstrators are built, preventing national delay from stalling EU standardization.
Institute a strict go/no-go gate at month 16 with a credible fallback plan if AltID traction remains weak.
review: Core tension: balancing rapid demonstrations with regulatory alignment; weakness: the go/no-go gate could be used to stall progress if commitments waver.

name: Dissemination — EU and policy engagement to normalize platform-neutral fallback language
lever_classification: dissemination — how external audiences are engaged
consequences: Expanded legitimacy and external pressure can accelerate reforms, while also exposing the project to EU-level scrutiny and disparate policy timelines. Downstream, if domestic traction remains weak, EU engagement could highlight misalignment and create political friction.
options: Publish a phased advocacy brief series targeting Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, Folketinget, and EU working groups to legitimize platform-neutral approaches.
Organize quarterly stakeholder roundtables with civil society and privacy researchers to translate technical findings into policy language and procurement criteria.
Secure formal EU feedback channels and contribute to European Digital Identity Wallet material with aligned terminology and demonstrator results.
review: Core tension: external legitimacy pressures may outpace domestic procurement reforms; weakness: options may overestimate EU appetite for platform-neutral requirements in the near term.

name: Demonstrator design — build two non-production demonstrators as credible evidence
lever_classification: product — what this lever enables (demonstrator development)
consequences: Two demonstrators provide concrete, independent evidence that standards-based fallback can work without Apple/Google trust anchors. Downstream, this increases credibility for procurement language but raises technical maintenance needs and may create expectations misaligned with eventual production readiness.
options: Develop a GrapheneOS mobile demonstrator that authenticates using FIDO2/WebAuthn with a hardware-bound credential in a sandbox public-service environment.
Create a browser-based fallback demonstrator using WebAuthn and OpenID Connect that runs independently of mobile OS platform trust signals.
Prototype a hardware-token-based fallback demonstrator using standard USB/NFC tokens in a standalone workstation scenario to illustrate platform-neutral access.
review: Core tension: demonstrations risk creating expectations beyond what procurement can deliver; weakness: the options assume the fallback path remains framework-compatible without full certification.

name: Operations — resilient staffing and risk governance for continuity
lever_classification: operations — how to structure the team and risk processes
consequences: A distributed, multi-institution team reduces single points of failure and improves continuity across phase handoffs. Downstream, it raises overhead, coordination complexity, and potential fragmentation unless governance norms are strongly established.
options: Adopt a distributed, multi-institution staffing model with shared security clearance and rotation to avoid single-point expertise.
Develop formal risk and compliance playbooks covering Danish and EU frameworks (NSIS, eIDAS, data handling) and incident response planning.
Create a contingency budgeting and contract-management plan to preserve momentum when phase funding becomes uncertain and external reviews require additional time.
review: Core tension: governance rigidity can slow adaptation; weakness: options may not secure executive sponsorship needed to sustain funding across all phases.

name: Procurement language alignment — embed platform-neutral fallback criteria within AltID-related tenders and supplier contracts
lever_classification: governance — how procurement terms enforce platform-neutral fallback
consequences: Direct effect: procurement terms force consideration of fallback in supplier design, shaping how vendors structure authentication flows. Downstream, this increases negotiation complexity and may slow procurement cycles, potentially provoking pushback from incumbents who rely on existing platform telemetry and certification models.
options: Embed explicit platform-neutral fallback requirements in AltID tender documents, including tests for independence from Apple/Google distribution channels and device telemetry, with a sunset clause for platform dependence.
Require supplier-submitted fallback architecture diagrams and third-party attestations showing how authentication would function without platform telemetry.
Incorporate procurement gating with staged milestones tied to open standard compliance and external security review before each budget release.
review: Core tension: aligning resilience goals with procurement power and supplier leverage; Weakness: the options do not specify enforcement mechanisms or penalties if sunset provisions or milestones are missed.

name: Interoperability standards enforcers — mandate OpenID Connect and WebAuthn baselines for essential services to reduce platform lock-in
lever_classification: methodology — which standards to mandate for interoperability across identity surfaces
consequences: Direct effect: pushes services toward shared, vendor-agnostic authentication baselines, creating a concrete fallback path. Downstream, this can raise integration costs and slow modernization if legacy systems resist rapid standard adoption.
options: Mandate OpenID Connect as the standard authentication protocol across all core public services, paired with WebAuthn for device-based authentication, with a regular cadence of conformance reviews.
Require portable identity tokens that work across multiple providers and devices, ensuring a certified fallback path remains usable during platform outages.
Institute a standards-based impact assessment per service to measure interoperability gaps and map dependencies on Apple/Google signals.
review: Core tension: standards adoption may disrupt legacy integrations; Weakness: no plan for transitional support or phased decommissioning of incompatible components.

name: Independent validation framework — commission external audits and public reporting on the credibility and risks of fallback paths
lever_classification: execution — how to validate and publicly report fallback evidence
consequences: Direct effect: external validation increases legitimacy and stakeholder confidence; downstream, it requires budget for independent work and robust data governance, and findings could constrain timelines if issues are flagged.
options: Contract an independent security lab to reproduce the fallback authentication workflow and publish a publicly accessible risk assessment and mitigations.
Establish a rotating panel of civil-society and academic reviewers to critique the demonstrators and provide gatekeeping for policy recommendations.
Create an open-data portal with standardized telemetry from demonstrators while ensuring privacy, enabling external verification by stakeholders.
review: Core tension: independence benefits credibility but may slow decision-making; Weakness: lacks guardrails for sensitive findings and national-security considerations.

name: Coalition governance — establish an interagency forum to supervise platform-neutral transition, procurement, and continuity planning
lever_classification: governance — who coordinates and approves platform-neutral initiatives
consequences: Direct effect: clarifies roles, decision gates, and accountability across ministries; downstream, it can enhance legitimacy but may add bureaucratic friction and slower momentum if conflicts arise.
options: Create a standing interagency council with clear decision gates to approve procurement language, standards, and demonstrator milestones, meeting monthly.
Formalize a rights and responsibilities charter with private-sector observers, ensuring transparency while protecting sensitive security information.
Launch a phased escalation protocol that triggers additional authorities when milestones slip, including parliamentary reporting requirements.
review: Core tension: coordinating multiple agencies against tight timelines; Weakness: options do not specify funding mechanisms or dispute-resolution processes when milestones slip.

name: Strategic procurement pathfinder — create phased, contract-structured pilots that tie fallback readiness milestones to budgetary releases
lever_classification: execution — how to structure funding and procurement milestones
consequences: Direct effect: aligns funding with demonstrable progress, incentivizing credible fallback work; downstream, it may slow funding if external reviews lag, and could incentivize scope-minimization to meet milestones.
options: Structure pilot contracts with milestone-based funding, where each phase unlocks continued funding only after validated fallback demonstrations.
Offer open-source reference implementations of fallback authentication to reduce vendor lock-in and accelerate conformance testing.
Incorporate a de-risking clause that allows rapid reallocation of funds if external reviews identify material non-conformities.
review: Core tension: funding discipline vs. agility; Weakness: lacks contingency paths if vendor performance or regulatory feedback delays milestones.

name: Public communications framework — publish plain-language analyses on sovereignty, resilience, and trade-offs to build legitimacy and counter opposition
lever_classification: dissemination — how to communicate the platform-neutral rationale and risks
consequences: Direct effect: increases transparency and public trust; downstream, it raises exposure to political shifts and misinterpretation, requiring sustained communications capacity and risk monitoring.
options: Publish quarterly reports detailing platform-dependency risks and the projected resilience gains from platform-neutral alternatives, with accessible summaries for citizens.
Host transparent open-houses and stakeholder forums to gather feedback and publicly refresh the coalition's mandate.
Develop a risk-communication playbook to address misinformation while outlining concrete timelines, costs, and decision rights.
review: Core tension: transparency can provoke political pushback; Weakness: lacks metrics for public understanding or ways to adapt messaging to diverse audiences.

name: Platform-Neutral Fallback Gatekeeping in AltID Procurement
lever_classification: governance — how procurement gates enforce platform-neutral fallback
consequences: Procurement processes will explicitly require platform-neutral fallback capabilities, linking them to acceptance criteria and milestones. This shift reduces the risk of vendor lock-in by ensuring fallback readiness is evaluated upfront, but it can extend sourcing timelines and raise initial cost estimates as new compliance checks are added. Downstream, agencies will need enhanced contract governance and technical evaluation capacity to manage more complex supplier assessments.
options: Integrate platform-neutral fallback requirements into every AltID-related RFP and contract through explicit milestones and acceptance criteria.
Institute a formal evaluation framework that weights interoperability, vendor diversity, and non-platform-dependent signals during procurement decisions.
Mandate pilot projects in at least three agencies to test fallback authentication paths before any live MitID integration is contemplated.
review: Core tension: pushing platform-neutral fallback into procurement can delay contracts and increase compliance overhead; weakness: options do not specify how to quantify risk or handle political pushback from platform owners.

name: Credible Demonstration Program for Platform-Neutral Access
consequences: Independent demonstrations provide tangible evidence of feasibility, strengthening political and public confidence in fallback approaches. This reduces perceived risk and can unlock policy momentum, but it concentrates effort and funding on non-production proofs which may not translate to immediate deployment readiness. Over time, credible demonstrations can shape procurement expectations and regulatory dialogue.
options: Sponsor a government-led demonstration program that runs in parallel to existing MitID channels, using open standards to validate interoperability and resilience.
Create an independent validation hub with third-party security experts evaluating the demonstrators against threat models and publish results openly.
Forge a cross-border collaboration to benchmark fallback architectures with EU peers and publish a joint maturity roadmap.
review: Core tension: credibility hinges on external validators; weakness: options rely on ad hoc funding and do not specify how validation findings influence procurement or certification timelines.

name: Continuity Drills and Disaster Scenario Exercises
lever_classification: operations — how to test resilience and continuity
consequences: Regular drills reveal operational gaps and validate the practicality of fallback paths under real-world stress, creating actionable remediation plans. This improves preparedness across agencies and reduces the time to recover during outages; however, it may expose budgetary and governance gaps that require additional funding or policy changes. The exercises also help align decision rights and escalation procedures in crisis situations.
options: Institutionalize quarterly survivability drills across critical public services to stress-test fallback authentication under governance constraints.
Run a cross-agency continuity exercise simulating platform outages with a temporary fallback path to measure user impact and recovery time.
Publish a transparent playbook detailing trigger conditions, decision rights, and escalation paths for continuity events.
review: Core tension: drills reveal real capacity gaps; weakness: exercises may not cover long-term upgrade cycles or procurement constraints.

name: EU-Aligned Interoperability and Standards Pressure for Fallback Authentication
lever_classification: dissemination — how policy and standards are shaped
consequences: Aligning Denmark with EU standards increases legitimacy for platform-neutral paths and facilitates cross-border use, reducing sovereign risk from vendor lock-in. It also slows progress due to diplomatic negotiation and competing national interests, and requires sustained resources for policy engagement. In the long run, this can unlock broader adoption and accelerated certification, improving resilience across services.
options: Advocate for an EU-wide standardization track on platform-neutral access in the Digital Identity Wallet program and NSIS alignment.
Lead a Danish EU working group to influence interoperability requirements and fallback certification criteria.
Sponsor a peer-review process with other member states to benchmark fallback architectures against common threat models.
review: Core tension: policy alignment is slow and complex; weakness: options assume consensus across member states, which is uncertain and may stall action.

name: Cross-Agency Risk-Sharing Consortium for Supplier Concentration
lever_classification: governance — who shares risk and oversight
consequences: A formal risk-sharing structure spreads procurement and operational risk, lowering the chance that a single agency bears disproportionate cost or delay. It also adds governance overhead and requires legal coordination across ministries, potentially creating slower decision cycles. The arrangement can incentivize supplier diversification but may face political resistance and budgetary alignment challenges.
options: Formation of a cross-agency risk-sharing consortium with joint procurement risk, liability modeling, and joint funding for fallback research.
Negotiate pooled liability agreements with default risk sharing to deter individual agency cost overruns.
Create a confidential vendor-relations forum to uncover anti-competitive risks and encourage alternative suppliers.
review: Core tension: risk pooling can dampen incentives for rapid action; weakness: may require new legal frameworks and long lead times to implement.

name: Copenhagen Secure Lab and Open Repository for Fallback Architectures
lever_classification: methodology — how to validate and document the technical approaches
consequences: A dedicated facility and public repository enhance transparency, enable independent validation, and accelerate knowledge diffusion across EU partners. The openness supports credibility but creates security considerations around sensitive details and blueprint secrecy. Proper governance and access controls are needed to balance transparency with protection of critical information.
options: Establish a Copenhagen-based secure lab to host controlled demonstrations, with a public repository of architecture diagrams and white papers.
Open-select research access to international partners under NDA to accelerate independent validation and ensure credibility.
Publish a living blueprint for a platform-neutral fallback that can be adapted by other EU states.
review: Core tension: openness versus security; weakness: must implement robust access governance and guard against sensitive data exposure.
